title: Teaching
text:
I am a teaching assistant in Geography department at the University of Oregon. I am particularly proud of the role I served as lab instructor for Remote Sensing I. In this course I developed lab material to teach students how to acquire imagery, manipulate and enhance it, classify it, and quantitatively analyze it.
Not only do these labs introduce remote sensing concepts to students, but more importantly, they show students how to use GIS software for certain tasks. See lab exercises below.

title: Constrast stretching
text:
Let’s read in a raster and set a color palette to visualize by. This example file is a modified normalized difference water index image within interior Alaska.

library(terra)

terra 1.8.5



colors <- colorRampPalette(c("white", "lightblue", 
                             "blue", "darkblue", "black"))(100)
r <- rast("../FullComp_Dalton_2015.tif", lyr = 24)
plot(r, col = colors)








Looks great. Now if we want to increase the contrast between different pixel values. We can do this

# contrast stretch
p_low <- quantile(values(r), 0.05, na.rm = TRUE)
p_high <- quantile(values(r), 0.95, na.rm = TRUE) 

r_stretched <- clamp((r - p_low) / (p_high - p_low), lower = 0, upper = 1)
plot(r_stretched, col = colors)

terra 1.8.5

title: Mosaic rasters

# dir and outdir
file_dir <- "/Users/wancher/Documents/weranch/code/tiffs/"

list_of_files <- list.files(file_dir, pattern = "S1A-.*\\.tif$", full.names = TRUE)

title: Mosaic rasters
section: Commenting out the for loop and just work with files from one year for this example
text:
Commenting out the for loop and just work with files from one year for this example

# iterate over a sequence of years and pull out files specific to the year in the sequence
# mosaics <- list()
# years <- seq(2019, 2023)
# years_string <- as.character(years)

#   year <- years_string[[i]]
#   files_one_year <- list_of_files[grepl(year, list_of_files)]
  files_one_year <- list_of_files

  
  list_of_rasters <- lapply(files_one_year, function(file) {
    r <- rast(file)
    r
  })
  
  # get band names for retention
  #band_names <- names(list_of_rasters[[1]])
  #flattened_band_names <- unlist(band_names)
  
  # turn list in sprc and mosaic
  coll_of_rasters <- sprc(list_of_rasters)
  #print(paste0(year, " start: ", Sys.time()))
  mosaiced_raster <- merge(coll_of_rasters) 


|---------|---------|---------|---------|

text:
Visualize

#without mosaicing
for (r in list_of_rasters){
  plot(r)
}





































#after mosaic
plot(mosaiced_raster, main = "Summer 2023 C Band Composite")

title: Lab three instructions
text:
If you have been using ArcGIS, it is recommended to use QGIS for this lab.




You are provided with two Landsat images of the Portland metro and surrounding areas from two different image dates. The images are located in the shared folder of the class R drive. Your task is to classify pixels in the Landsat 8 image to match a classification schema of interest (e.g., land cover or forest type). You will use Google Earth for ground truth or “training” data in the classification workflow.
This lab is divided into three parts: 1. Unsupervised and Supervised Classification: Implement two different classification techniques and examine their advantages and disadvantages. 2. Accuracy Assessment: Compare your classification output to your training data to assess accuracy. 3. Change Detection Analysis: Apply the steps to the second Landsat image and assess landscape changes both qualitatively and quantitatively.







What would you like to investigate about the landscape?
Create a classification key with class, value, and color as columns for reference.
Insert your classification key here (screenshot or table).






Load Bands into QGIS:

Load all bands into QGIS.
Build a true-color composite or create a virtual raster for reference.
Set the resolution to the highest possible and place input files into separate bands.

Insert screenshot of your true-color composite in QGIS here.
Create a Region of Interest (ROI):

Zoom in to a specific region of interest.
Create a new shapefile layer:

Go to Layer → Create Layer → New Shapefile Layer. Select polygon geometry and specify a file name.

Add features to your shapefile by selecting the pencil icon and drawing a box around your area of interest.
Right-click to stop editing, enter 1 for the ID, and save edits.

Insert screenshot of your shapefile and drawn ROI here.
Clip Raster by Mask Layer:

Search for Clip Raster by Mask Layer in the processing toolbox.
Input layers: Raw band images (red, green, blue).
Mask layer: The shapefile you just created.
Run the tool once for each input layer.

Insert screenshot of clipped raster layers here.
Run K-Means Clustering:

Search for K-means clustering for grids in the processing toolbox (equivalent to Iso Cluster Unsupervised Classification in ArcGIS).
Input “grids”: The clipped/masked bands (red, green, blue).
Set the number of clusters to match the classes in your key.
Define the number of iterations (consider experimenting with different values).
Press Run and export the resulting layer as a TIF.

Insert screenshot of the clustering process and output layer here.
Adjust Symbology and Validate:

Change the symbology of the output layer to paletted/unique values and use a random color ramp.
Validate classification by comparing with your true color composite. Toggle the layers to inspect.

Insert screenshot of output with color ramp and validation process here.






Obtain Training Data:

You can generate random points in the layer bounds.

Input layer: The bounding box shapefile from step 2.
The number of points should be at least 10-15 points per class.


Insert screenshot of random points or training points here.
Label Points in Google Earth:

Open Google Earth and import your KML file (if using random points).
Label your points using your classification key based on land feature interpretation.
If the random points are not well-placed, manually adjust them in Google Earth.

Insert screenshot of labeled points in Google Earth here.
Export Training Data:

Right-click the KML layer in Google Earth and export it.
Import the KML file into QGIS and export it as a shapefile. Make sure the file name is clear (e.g., train_points_10classes_10obs.shp).

Insert screenshot of shapefile with training points here.
Add a Value Column:

Open the attribute table of your training points shapefile.
Add a new field to assign numerical values to your classes.

Insert screenshot of the attribute table and new field here.
Install dzetsaka Plugin:

Install the dzetsaka classification tool by going to Plugins → Manage and Install Plugins. Search for dzetsaka and install it.
In the Python Console, type:
import pip
pip.main(['install', 'scikit-learn'])

Insert screenshot of dzetsaka plugin installation here.
Train Classifier:

Under the dzetsaka classification tool, select Train Algorithm.
Input raster: The raster you want the model to learn (e.g., true-color composite).
Input layer: Your training points shapefile. Set the field to the column with numerical value assignments.
Select the K-nearest neighbors algorithm (start with this one).
Set the pixel percentage for validation to 20% and specify the output model path.

Insert screenshot of classifier training setup here.
Predict Classifications:

After training, select Predict Model under the dzetsaka tool.
Input raster: The same raster you trained the model with.
Specify the output raster file name and ensure it’s saved as a .tif file.

Insert screenshot of classification output prediction here.






What are you trying to classify? Insert your response here.
Which technique performed better and why? Insert your response here.
What is the k-means clustering algorithm doing? And the K-nearest neighbors algorithm? Insert your response here.
What biases could be introduced in the training process? How was the training data distributed spatially? How many observations per class? Insert your response here.







Generate a Confusion Matrix:

To assess accuracy, you need to generate a confusion matrix. You may need to install the Orfeo ToolBox plugin for this.
Install it by downloading from Orfeo ToolBox and placing it in the C:/Program Files/QGIS directory.
Search for ComputeConfusionMatrix and set the input image to your classified image, with ground truth set to your training points.

Insert screenshot of confusion matrix for both supervised and unsupervised classification here.
Report Accuracy Metrics:

Calculate and report:

Overall Accuracy
Producers Accuracy
Users Accuracy


Insert your confusion matrix results and interpretation here.





What were your accuracy results? What does each statistic tell you? Insert your response here.
Which classification technique performed better? Insert your response here.
What do you think was the greatest flaw in your classification workflow? Insert your response here.
Which classes are underclassified and overclassified? Insert your response here.
Are there consistencies in accuracy between supervised and unsupervised methods? Insert your response here.
How might the accuracy of your classification be improved? Insert your response here.
What type of classification technique are supervised and unsupervised methods? Insert your response here.
What other types of classification techniques are there? Insert your response here.







Perform Change Detection:
Use the same process to perform post-classification change detection with the second Landsat image. Look for changes in land cover such as forest to non-forest or grassland to urban.
Investigate changes in terms of percent area or proportion of the landscape. Quantify the change in m² or hectares and assess the implications.
Insert screenshot of change detection output here.





Reflect on the findings from each part of the lab, the effectiveness of the classification techniques, and the insights gained from the change detection analysis.

title: Lab one instructions
text:
Get software working and a reliable place to save files.
Inspect, display, and compare at least 5 types of remote sensing images.
Acquaint yourselves with common terms that refer to image attributes and phenomena.




For this lab, you will need: - QGIS (which should be installed on machines in SSIL) - EarthExplorer (requires an account)




Set up a directory:

Create a directory where you will save your files for this class. Consider using Dropbox or Google Drive for easy access and backup.

Form groups:

If you prefer, form groups and track how the labor is divided. Each student will need to submit their work as a PDF.

Select a study area:

Choose a study area within the US (preferably CONUS). Make sure it’s an area you wouldn’t mind revisiting in future labs.

Download 5 types of remotely sensed images:

Select images from different sensors (e.g., Landsat, aerial imagery, or other satellites).
You can use EarthExplorer or other reliable sources for downloading.
Be sure to download the metadata for each image if available.

Use Google and Online Resources:

Google is your friend! Utilize resources like Stack Exchange GIS Community for troubleshooting.







Your final submission should be a PDF containing:

Screenshot of each image with brief description
Discuss trade offs between images
A brief reflection on the types of imagery you would use for specific studies

title: Lab two instructions
text:
In your group, you should use your Region of Interest (ROI) to acquire satellite data. Follow the steps below:

Download Data: First, download your satellite data from Earth Explorer. Drop a pin as close to your ROI as possible.
Landsat Scenes: You need to download TWO Landsat scenes from different time periods (at least 10 years apart). Be sure to download all the bands and metadata. This will help in understanding the details of the imagery and provide extra information when needed.
Import Data into QGIS: Once the data is downloaded, bring the Landsat scenes into QGIS for further analysis.




Here are some techniques you can apply in QGIS using the symbology pane:



What is Contrast Stretching?
What parameters did you change and what was the result?





What color ramps did you experiment with?





What is Pan Sharpening?
Did you try pan sharpening?





Did you try any high or low pass filtering?





What is a Spectral Index?
Why did you choose the spectral index you used?
How did you perform band math in QGIS?





Your submission should include: - An introduction to your study area and the analysis you are interested in. - Any images you generated or manipulated from the raw data that you found helpful (include screenshots). - A brief explanation of the techniques you used to enhance the imagery, including how these techniques work, how you implemented them in QGIS, and the insights you gained.
The final report should be submitted as a PDF.




What is pan sharpening? Contrast stretching?

What parameters did you change and what was the result?

What is a spectral index? Why is the spectral index you chose relevant for your region of interest?
How do you perform band math in QGIS?
Did you try any filtering or smoothing?

title: Maps and such
text:
This is the landscape in interior Alaska which my thesis is focused on. It is a roughly 8 million hectare landscape (black outline) fixed within the Alaska and Brooks mountain ranges.

Here is my take on a change detection map. This map is neat because the time step and species visualized can be adjusted based on your data.

title: Graphing raster distribution
text:
Histograms are the best way to get a glimpse of data, no? This code is useful if you have a multiband raster stack and want to plot the data distribution on a normalized scale. My output from this got a lot of thumbs ups at AGU this year.

library(terra)

terra 1.8.5

library(tidyverse)

── Attaching core tidyverse packages ──────────────────────── tidyverse 2.0.0 ──
✔ dplyr     1.1.4     ✔ readr     2.1.5
✔ forcats   1.0.0     ✔ stringr   1.5.1
✔ ggplot2   3.5.1     ✔ tibble    3.2.1
✔ lubridate 1.9.4     ✔ tidyr     1.3.1
✔ purrr     1.0.2     


── Conflicts ────────────────────────────────────────── tidyverse_conflicts() ──
✖ tidyr::extract() masks terra::extract()
✖ dplyr::filter()  masks stats::filter()
✖ dplyr::lag()     masks stats::lag()
ℹ Use the conflicted package (<http://conflicted.r-lib.org/>) to force all conflicts to become errors

library(ggridges)
library(dplyr)
library(RColorBrewer)

# path to your multiband raster
path <- "../FullComp_Dalton_2015.tif"


#example file
r <- rast(path)
band_names <- names(r)
normalize_layer <- function(layer) {
  min_val <- min(layer[], na.rm = TRUE)
  max_val <- max(layer[], na.rm = TRUE)
  normalized <- (layer - min_val) / (max_val - min_val)
  return(normalized)
}

normalized_rasters <- lapply(1:nlyr(r), function(i) {
  band <- r[[i]]
  normalized_band <- normalize_layer(band)
  return(normalized_band)
})

# stack the normalized rasters
normalized_r <- rast(normalized_rasters)

Convert raster to df and plot it:

#convert to df
df <- as.data.frame(normalized_r, xy = FALSE, na.rm = TRUE)
df_long <- df %>%
  pivot_longer(cols = everything(), 
               names_to = "Band", 
               values_to = "Value")
df_long_sampled <- df_long %>%
  sample_n(500)

#ridge plot
#display.brewer.all()
colors <- colorRampPalette(brewer.pal(11, "Paired"))(57)  # 57 colors

plt <- ggplot(df_long_sampled, aes(x = Value, y = Band, fill = Band)) +
  geom_density_ridges_gradient(scale = 10, rel_min_height = 0.002, linewidth = 0.35) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = colors)+
  #scale_fill_viridis_d(option = "plasma") +  
  theme(
    axis.title = element_blank(),  
    axis.text.x = element_blank(),
    axis.text.y = element_text(size = 12, color = "black", face = "bold"),
    axis.ticks = element_blank()   
  )
#plt

Any band denoted with _1 is derived from a spring composite, _2, a summer composite, and _3, a fall composite. Hence, the color grouping in the plot. For context, these are layers that are used as inputs into a random forest model prior to remove correlated variables.

title: Raster manipulation using terra
text:
This R script reads in NDWI images derived the blue-red/blue+red equation, converts them to binary images using a threshold from the literature, and then removes outlier pixels which are disconnected from large water bodies.

# setting up
library(terra)

terra 1.8.5


Just working with one file in this example. But you can imagine reading in a list of files and performing this operation iteratively with a for loop.

r <- rast("../ndwi_ice_small.tif")
plot(r)








So this is what the NDWI image looks like. Let’s visualize as lake vs non lake.

lake_mask <- r > 0.25
plot(lake_mask)








This looks good but if you look at some of the isolated yellow pixels, they would then be considered a lake pixel even if disconnected from the larger lake. So we remove those.

# function to sieve
lake_sieve <- function(ndwi_thres_raster) {
  # get connected components
  connected_comp <- patches(ndwi_thres_raster, directions = 4, zeroAsNA = TRUE)
  components <- unique(values(connected_comp), na.rm = TRUE)
  components <- components[components != 0 & !is.na(components)]
  cell_indices <- unique(values(connected_comp, na.rm = TRUE))
  # empty mask for valid lakes
  valid_lake_mask <- rast(ndwi_thres_raster)
  values(valid_lake_mask) <- 0
  min_pixel <- 10
  min_width <- 1
  #loop over each ndwi scene
  for (comp_id in components) {
    # get cell indices for the current cc
    cell_indices <- which(values(connected_comp) == comp_id)
    if (length(cell_indices) < min_pixel) next
    # convert cell indices to coordinates
    coords <- xyFromCell(ndwi_thres_raster, cell_indices)
    width <- length(unique(coords[, "x"]))
    height <- length(unique(coords[, "y"]))
    # check if the component meets width/height requirements
    if (width <= min_width || height <= min_width) next
    values(valid_lake_mask)[cell_indices] <- 1 #update valid lake
  }
  return(valid_lake_mask)
}

Apply the function

sieved_r <- lake_sieve(lake_mask)
plot(sieved_r)








Looking good!! This is just a qualitative sieving technique and could easily adapt to more stats-based approaches. This layer can now be used to mask other rasters to. In my approach, I mask images in the red and panchromatic wavelengths by this image to isolate lakes and apply radiative transfer equations.

title: Wesley Rancher
text:
I’m a master’s student at the University of Oregon, where I use spatial data science to tackle questions about climate change and forest ecology. My thesis focuses on mapping and modeling above-ground carbon density in Interior Alaska with Landsat imagery, random forests, and a forest simulation model, LANDIS-II.
I bring experience in hydrology, forest ecology, and geospatial analysis using tools like Google Earth Engine, R, Python, and ArcGIS. I’ve developed cloud-based data processing algorithms, and conducted simulation modeling and machine learning in HPC environments using the AWS CLI, Bash, and Docker.
On this site, you’ll find:

Reproducible code examples for spatial data and statistical analyses.
Teaching materials I’ve developed to help students apply remote sensing concepts.
Maps, posters, and publications as my form of a portfolio.



M.S. in Geography (In progress) University of Oregon | Eugene, OR
B.A. in Environmental Studies, Geography, and Philosophy Ohio Wesleyan University | Delaware, OH

title: Wesley Rancher
text:
M.S. in Geography (In progress) University of Oregon | Eugene, OR
B.A. in Environmental Studies, Geography, and Philosophy Ohio Wesleyan University | Delaware, OH

title: maps!
text:
AOI map for thesis



Change detection map idea



Change detection reclass map of total carbon in the year 2100. Split by diagonal line in the middle. The left is no climate change the right is extreme climate change. This was a fun exercise of patience in R, attempting to draw a line at the right location with the right slope to mask my images by.




LANDIS-II Output Maps



Comparison of number of simulated fires in LANDIS-II across three distinct climate scenarios. From top down: no climate change, moderate climate change, extreme climate change




Landsat Composite



Sometimes imagery without context can be visually pleasing. This is a composite image within Interior Alaska where tasseled cap brightness is displayed in the red channel, greenness in green, and wetness in the blue channel.

title: Wesley Rancher
text:
Department of Geography, University of Oregon
Terrestrial Ecosystems Ecology and Landscapes Lab
Email: wesr@uoregon.edu | Linkedin | github



Biogeographer specializing in spatial data science and ecology, with a focus on understanding ecosystem dynamics and environmental change across diverse landscapes and time scales. Skilled in GIS, remote sensing, and spatial modeling, and passionate about leveraging these tools to address pressing environmental problems and communicate research findings to the public. Eager to contribute to research and industry initiatives that support data-driven climate solutions and enhance ecological and social resilience.






M.S. in Geography
GPA: 3.9
Advisor: Dr. Melissa Lucash



B.A. in Environmental Studies and Geography
Minor in Philosophy
GPA: 3.5
Advisor: Dr. Nathan Rowley






GIS & Remote Sensing: Advanced in ArcGIS, QGIS, Google Earth Engine

Programming: Advanced in R and Python; proficient in Bash and GitHub

Drone Operation & Image Processing: FAA Certified Remote UAS Pilot (#4802988); experience with Pix4D, Drone2Map, Agisoft; proficient in LiDAR and Dual Red-Edge sensors calibration

Languages: Working proficiency in Spanish







September 2023 – Present
Advisor: Dr. Melissa Lucash
- Quantified aboveground carbon dynamics in boreal Alaska using remote sensing and simulation modeling
- Wrote code in Python API for Google Earth Engine to perform atmospheric correction, cross-sensor calibration, and spectral index calculations
- Integrated remote sensing data, and climate projections with LANDIS-II model to simulate ecosystem change in boreal Alaska
- Analyzed large climate datasets from CMIP5 and CMIP6



June 2023 – August 2023
Advisor: Dr. Anthony Vorster
- Collaborated with Grand Staircase Escalante Partners to map invasive plant communities in the Paria River Watershed, Utah
- Processed large datasets using Google Earth Engine, R, and ArcGIS



December 2022 – May 2023
Advisor: Dr. Nathan Rowley
- Estimated supraglacial lake depth development in Western Greenland using radiative transfer models
- Developed workflows in Google Earth Engine for Landsat data acquisition



June 2022 – July 2022
Advisor: Dr. Victor Gonzalez
- Analyzed climate stressors on heat tolerances of honeybees and sweat bees in Lesvos, Greece
- Discovered that bees remain heat tolerant following desiccation and starvation







September 2023 – Present
Lab Instructor – Geography 485/585: Remote Sensing I (Fall, Winter 2025)
Developed lab exercises, taught GIS and remote sensing software, and provided virtual and in-person demonstrations
Course Assistant – Geography 199: Global Wildfire (Spring 2024)
Supported curriculum development and provided supplemental instruction
Guest Lecturer
Geography 199: “Changing Wildfire in Brazil”
Geography 199: “Bees and Wildfire”
Lab Instructor – Geography 181: Our Digital Earth (Fall 2023, Winter 2024)
Facilitated labs on digital mapping and spatial data






Rippey Research Grant ($1000, UO) – 2024

NASA Develop Scholarship ($1500, SSAI) – 2023

Dean’s List (OWU) – Fall ’22, Spring ’20, ’22, ’23

Robert E. Shanklin Distinguished Scholar (Geography, OWU) – 2023

Phi Sigma Tau (Philosophy, OWU) – 2023

Our New Gold Digital Storytelling Winner (Spanish, OWU) – 2022
